body_txt: The trend of youths consuming offensive and controversial media created from street culture led to a bit of a problem for producers who wanted to make a wholesome show for the whole family. How were they supposed to appeal to these disaffected youths that made up a large chunk of their target audience? Enter the '90s Bad Boy: a chaotic troublemaker who had all the image of street culture, but who rarely did much worse than cutting class or pulling pranks and thus could still settle in nicely with a quirky Dom Com family. In fact, the '90s Bad Boy makes a great main character in the comedy genre, as his antics drives the plots. In most cases, he often comes up with a Zany Scheme and gets himself and sometimes others into wacky situations as a result. Many of these follow the example of the Trope Codifier , Bart Simpson from The Simpsons , who made a massive impact in the form of Bartmania and whose too-cool-for-school personality essentially gave birth to the Attitude Era. As a result, there was a trend of mischievous , rebellious , smart-alecky , Book Dumb yet Street Smart and Fun Personified troublemakers who go against the social grain in order to strive to follow the Rule of Cool / Fun . The recipe for one of these boys is fairly simple. You'll just have to pull some traits from three broad categories: A sub-trope of Totally Radical and Teen Rebellion that overlaps with The Troublemaker . Compare Stock Shōnen Hero (an often Chaotic Good hero archetype with spiky hair and a Hot-Blooded , mischievous and rebellious personality), Fratbro (a college-aged student who likes parties and sex), Greaser Delinquents (who rebel against social norms to prove their willpower and strength) and Japanese Delinquents (same). Also see Troublemaker Hero if the protagonist has a tendency of getting into mischief, Mascot with Attitude , a similar archetype that thrives in video games and whose cool personality is marketed toward youth, and '90s Anti-Hero for the "edgy" comic book equivalent of the archetype. It is also a Distaff Counterpart to Gamer Chick . Contrast with Awkward Relatable Schoolkid , a dorky kid who is trying to find his place, and The All-American Boy , a chronological predecessor of the archetype, often times the '90s bad boy's father in their childhood, who is still expected to get up to mischief, but in a "wholesome" way; the transition between the two occurred when simplified, "All-American" portrayals of town and family life largely fell out of favor around the end of the Cold War era, with the adventurous, mischievous preteen boy becoming cast as a more irreverent and rebellious figure and picking up interests in "edgy" and countercultural pastimes while still remaining essentially non-malicious.
